NYC Yellow Taxi Fleet Positioning System¶

Group 10 - BUDT 758J: Enterprise Cloud Computing and Big Data¶

Robert H. Smith School of Business | Spring 2026¶

Business Context¶

Decision Maker: A NYC Yellow Taxi fleet manager responsible for repositioning idle cabs across the city's 263 taxi zones in real time.

Core Question: Given the current time of day, weather conditions, scheduled public events, and neighbourhood complaint patterns - which zones need more cabs right now, and which need fewer?

Fleet managers don't just need a demand forecast instead they need directional, actionable guidance: move cabs from Zone A to Zone B when it rains; pre-position near Zone C an hour before a large event ends. Our analysis is built around that decision, not just prediction accuracy.

Data Sources¶

# Dataset Source Key Variables
1 NYC Yellow Taxi Trip Records (2025) NYC TLC Pickup zone, timestamp to hourly demand counts
2 Hourly Weather (2025) Open-Meteo Archive API Temperature, precipitation, weather code
3 NYC Taxi Zone Shapefiles NYC TLC Zone boundaries (263 polygons), borough names
4 Special Events Permits (2025) NYC Open Data Event name, type, borough, scheduled dates
5 311 Service Complaints (2025) NYC Open Data Complaint type, descriptor text, coordinates

Analytical Approach¶

  1. Data Acquisition: Five public data sources pulled via REST APIs and cloud file storage
  2. Text Analysis: Classify event permits by type using tokenization and TF-IDF
  3. Sentiment Analysis: Score 311 complaint severity from free-text descriptions using VADER
  4. Geocoding: Map complaint and event locations to taxi zones using spatial joins
  5. Exploratory Analysis: Understand demand patterns across time, weather, events, and complaints
  6. Demand Modelling: Quantify how much each external data source improves predictions
  7. Repositioning Scores: Translate findings into actionable zone-level guidance
  8. AI-Powered Synthesis: Use a large language model to generate natural language recommendations

Part 0 - Environment Setup¶

We install two additional packages and import everything needed for the project. All data acquisition, text processing, geographic analysis, machine learning, and AI components are loaded here so the notebook can run cleanly from top to bottom.

In [1]:

All libraries loaded.

Connecting to Cloud Storage¶

Our 12 monthly taxi trip files (one per month, 2025) are stored in Google Drive. Mounting Drive gives us direct file access without re-uploading large files. The folder contains 15 items in total: The 12 parquet files plus the taxi zone shapefile zip and a couple of supporting project files.

In [3]:
from google.colab import drive
drive.mount('/content/drive')

BASE_PATH = '/content/drive/MyDrive/CloudComputingProject'
parquet_files = [f for f in os.listdir(BASE_PATH) if f.endswith('.parquet')]
print(f'Parquet files found: {len(parquet_files)}')
print(os.listdir(BASE_PATH))

Mounted at /content/drive
Parquet files found: 15
['yellow_tripdata_2025-01.parquet', 'yellow_tripdata_2025-02.parquet', 'yellow_tripdata_2025-03.parquet', 'yellow_tripdata_2025-04.parquet', 'yellow_tripdata_2025-05.parquet', 'yellow_tripdata_2025-06.parquet', 'yellow_tripdata_2025-07.parquet', 'yellow_tripdata_2025-08.parquet', 'yellow_tripdata_2025-09.parquet', 'yellow_tripdata_2025-10.parquet', 'yellow_tripdata_2025-11.parquet', 'yellow_tripdata_2025-12.parquet', 'taxi_zones.shp', 'taxi_zones', 'final_dataset_2025.csv', 'taxi_zones(1).zip', 'mta_2025.parquet', 'mta_2025_full.parquet', 'mta_partial.parquet', 'Python files', 'zone_demand_detailed.csv', 'Big Data Project.twbx', 'Final Submission']

Part 1 - Data Acquisition¶

Acquiring clean, reliable data from multiple sources is the foundation of this project. We pull from five distinct sources and validate each one before moving to the next step.

1.1 NYC Yellow Taxi Trip Records¶

The NYC Taxi & Limousine Commission publishes one parquet file per month containing every yellow cab trip. Each row is a single trip with a pickup datetime and a pickup zone ID (one of 263 zones).

Our aggregation logic:

  • Filter each file to records within the correct calendar month, removing misdated rows that appear occasionally in the raw data
  • Floor pickup timestamps to the hour; a fleet manager acts at the hourly level, not the individual trip level
  • Count trips per zone per hour becomes our demand variable

We define a reusable function first and test it on a single file before running the full loop across all 12 months.

In [4]:
# Define a function to process one monthly file.
def process_taxi_file(file_path):
    df = pd.read_parquet(file_path)
    df['tpep_pickup_datetime'] = pd.to_datetime(df['tpep_pickup_datetime'], errors='coerce')
    df = df.dropna(subset=['tpep_pickup_datetime'])

    # Extract expected month from filename and filter to it
    parts = os.path.basename(file_path).replace('.parquet','').split('_')[-1].split('-')
    year, month = parts[0], parts[1]
    start = f'{year}-{month}-01'
    end   = pd.to_datetime(start) + pd.DateOffset(months=1)
    df    = df[(df['tpep_pickup_datetime'] >= start) & (df['tpep_pickup_datetime'] < end)]

    df['pickup_hour'] = df['tpep_pickup_datetime'].dt.floor('h')
    return df.groupby(['pickup_hour','PULocationID']).size().reset_index(name='demand')

# Test on January before running the full loop
sample = process_taxi_file(os.path.join(BASE_PATH, 'yellow_tripdata_2025-01.parquet'))
print(f'January test: {len(sample):,} zone-hour records')
sample

taxi_files = sorted([f for f in os.listdir(BASE_PATH)
                     if f.startswith('yellow_tripdata_2025') and f.endswith('.parquet')])

all_months = []
for fname in taxi_files:
    print(f'  Processing {fname}...')
    all_months.append(process_taxi_file(os.path.join(BASE_PATH, fname)))

full_data = pd.concat(all_months, ignore_index=True)
full_data = full_data.rename(columns={'PULocationID': 'LocationID'})
full_data['date']        = full_data['pickup_hour'].dt.date
full_data['hour']        = full_data['pickup_hour'].dt.hour
full_data['day_of_week'] = full_data['pickup_hour'].dt.day_name()

print(f'\nTotal zone-hour records: {len(full_data):,}')
print(f'Date range: {full_data["pickup_hour"].min()} → {full_data["pickup_hour"].max()}')

1430274 rows × 6 columns

Taxi data validated. 262 unique zones appear across 365 days. One zone had zero recorded pickups all year, likely a restricted or water-adjacent area; this is expected and has no impact on the analysis. The demand distribution is heavily right-skewed: the mean is 34 trips per zone-hour but the median is just 4, meaning a small number of high-traffic zones (Midtown, airports) pull the average up sharply. The maximum of 1,300 trips in a single zone-hour corresponds to peak periods at major transit hubs and is not an error. We move to weather data next.

1.2 Weather Data - Open-Meteo REST API¶

Weather is one of the strongest external drivers of taxi demand. We retrieve hourly observations for all of 2025 from the Open-Meteo Historical Archive using a REST API call. Variables retrieved: temperature (°C), precipitation (mm), and WMO weather interpretation code.

In [7]:
def fetch_nyc_weather(start_date='2025-01-01', end_date='2025-12-31'):
    url    = 'https://archive-api.open-meteo.com/v1/archive'
    params = {
        'latitude': 40.7128, 'longitude': -74.0060,
        'start_date': start_date, 'end_date': end_date,
        'hourly': ['temperature_2m','precipitation','weather_code'],
        'timezone': 'America/New_York'
    }
    print('Calling Open-Meteo API...')
    r = requests.get(url, params=params)
    r.raise_for_status()
    hourly = r.json()['hourly']
    return pd.DataFrame({
        'pickup_hour':  pd.to_datetime(hourly['time']).floor('h'),
        'temp_c':       hourly['temperature_2m'],
        'precip_mm':    hourly['precipitation'],
        'weather_code': hourly['weather_code']
    })

weather_2025 = fetch_nyc_weather()
print(f'Records: {len(weather_2025):,} (expect 8,760 for full year)')
weather_2025

In [8]:
# Validate the weather data.
print('=== Weather Validation ===')
print(f'Missing values: {weather_2025.isna().sum().to_dict()}')
print(f'Temperature range: {weather_2025["temp_c"].min():.1f}°C to {weather_2025["temp_c"].max():.1f}°C')

=== Weather Validation ===
Missing values: {'pickup_hour': 0, 'temp_c': 0, 'precip_mm': 0, 'weather_code': 0}
Temperature range: -16.6°C to 40.4°C
Rainy hours (precip > 0): 1,209

Weather data is complete and clean. All 8,760 hourly records are present with no missing values. The temperature range of -16.6°C to 40.4°C reflects a full NYC year where both extremes are relevant, as very cold and very hot conditions both suppress walking and push riders toward cabs. Rain fell in 1,209 of 8,760 hours (roughly 14% of the year), giving us a meaningful rainy-hour sample for the demand comparisons in Part 5.

1.3 NYC Taxi Zone Shapefiles¶

The NYC TLC distributes a shapefile defining the exact polygon boundary of each of the 263 taxi zones. We load this using geopandas and reproject to WGS84 (EPSG:4326) so that coordinate systems are consistent with our other datasets.

This shapefile serves two purposes: it lets us attach zone names and borough labels to our demand data, and it is the reference geometry we use to geocode complaint and event locations in Part 3.

In [9]:
# Extract and load the shapefile.
zip_path     = os.path.join(BASE_PATH, 'taxi_zones(1).zip')
extract_path = os.path.join(BASE_PATH, 'taxi_zones')

if not os.path.exists(extract_path):
    with zipfile.ZipFile(zip_path, 'r') as z:
        z.extractall(extract_path)

zones = gpd.read_file(os.path.join(extract_path, 'taxi_zones', 'taxi_zones.shp'))
zones = zones[['LocationID','zone','borough','geometry']].to_crs(epsg=4326)

print(f'Zones loaded: {len(zones)} (expect 263)')
print(zones['borough'].value_counts().to_string())
zones

263 rows × 4 columns

Shapefile loaded correctly. All 263 zones are present. One zone: EWR (Newark Airport) is technically in New Jersey, but yellow cabs are permitted to pick up there and it is included in the standard TLC zone system. It accounts for very low trip volume and does not affect the analysis. The mix of POLYGON and MULTIPOLYGON geometry types is expected for zones that span water bodies or disconnected land areas.

1.4 NYC Special Events Permits¶

The NYC Open Data portal publishes all city-issued special event permits via a publicly accessible REST API. Each permit describes an event that required a formal city permit these include: street fairs, parades, marathons, film shoots, concerts, and more.

Key fields: event name and type (free text), the issuing borough, and scheduled start and end times. Note: This dataset does not include latitude/longitude coordinates for all records - we address the geographic assignment in Part 3 using the borough field.

We pull broadly (no date filter in the API query, which can cause silent failures) and filter to 2025 records in pandas.

In [10]:
# We fetch broadly and filter to 2025 in pandas — safer than SoQL date quoting.
def fetch_nyc_events(limit=50000):
    url    = 'https://data.cityofnewyork.us/resource/bkfu-528j.json'
    params = {'$limit': limit, '$order': 'start_date_time DESC'}
    print('Calling NYC Open Data events API...')
    r = requests.get(url, params=params)
    r.raise_for_status()
    df = pd.DataFrame(r.json())
    print(f'Total pulled: {len(df):,}')
    return df

events_raw_full = fetch_nyc_events()
print('Columns:', list(events_raw_full.columns))

Calling NYC Open Data events API...
Total pulled: 50,000
Columns: ['event_agency', 'event_id', 'event_name', 'start_date_time', 'end_date_time', 'event_type', 'event_borough', 'event_location', 'street_closure_type', 'community_board', 'police_precinct', 'event_street_side']
In [11]:
# Parse dates, filter to 2025, cast numeric fields.
events_raw_full['start_date_time'] = pd.to_datetime(events_raw_full['start_date_time'], errors='coerce')
events_raw_full = events_raw_full.dropna(subset=['start_date_time'])

events_raw = events_raw_full[events_raw_full['start_date_time'].dt.year == 2025].copy()
print(f'2025 events: {len(events_raw):,}')

for col in ['latitude','longitude','attendance']:
    events_raw[col] = pd.to_numeric(events_raw.get(col, np.nan), errors='coerce')

events_raw['event_hour'] = events_raw['start_date_time'].dt.floor('h')
for col in ['event_name','event_type']:
    if col in events_raw.columns:
        events_raw[col] = events_raw[col].fillna('').str.lower().str.strip()

print(f'Events with lat/lon: {events_raw["latitude"].notna().sum():,}')
print('By borough:'); print(events_raw['event_borough'].value_counts().to_string())

2025 events: 31,182
Events with lat/lon: 0
By borough:
event_borough
Manhattan        15501
Brooklyn          7645
Queens            5175
Staten Island     1660
Bronx             1201

Events data acquired. 31,182 permits for 2025, filtered from the 50,000-record API pull (which contains historical records beyond 2025). Manhattan accounts for roughly half of all events (15,501), with Brooklyn second (7,645) and the Bronx a distant last (1,201). This imbalance is worth noting: because we geocode events at the borough level, the Manhattan signal is spread across one representative zone despite covering a very diverse borough. The practical effect is that our event feature will be noisier for Manhattan than for smaller boroughs.

1.5 NYC 311 Service Complaints¶

The NYC 311 system records every resident service complaint with a complaint type, free-text descriptor, and geographic coordinates. We filter to complaint types that are adjacent to taxi operations; taxi and for-hire vehicle complaints, noise on streets and sidewalks, blocked driveways, illegal parking, and traffic signal conditions.

To ensure we capture all relevant complaints without an arbitrary cutoff, we paginate through the API in batches of 50,000 until the endpoint returns no further records. The events dataset by contrast fits entirely within a single 50,000-record call, so no pagination was needed there.

In [12]:
RELEVANT_COMPLAINT_TYPES = [
    'Taxi Complaint', 'For Hire Vehicle Complaint',
    'Noise - Street/Sidewalk', 'Noise - Commercial',
    'Blocked Driveway', 'Illegal Parking',
    'Traffic Signal Condition', 'Street Light Condition',
]

complaint_filter = ' OR '.join(
    [f"complaint_type='{ct}'" for ct in RELEVANT_COMPLAINT_TYPES]
)

def fetch_nyc_311_full(year=2025):
    """
    Pulls ALL taxi-adjacent 311 complaints for 2025 by paginating
    through the Socrata API in batches of 50,000 until no more records remain.
    This ensures we capture the complete dataset rather than an arbitrary sample.
    """
    url        = 'https://data.cityofnewyork.us/resource/erm2-nwe9.json'
    batch_size = 50000
    offset     = 0
    all_batches = []

    while True:
        params = {
            '$limit':  batch_size,
            '$offset': offset,
            '$where':  f"created_date >= '{year}-01-01T00:00:00' "
                       f"AND created_date < '{year+1}-01-01T00:00:00' "
                       f"AND ({complaint_filter})",
            '$select': 'unique_key,created_date,complaint_type,descriptor,borough,latitude,longitude',
            '$order':  'created_date ASC',
        }
        print(f'  Fetching records {offset:,} – {offset+batch_size:,}...')
        r = requests.get(url, params=params)
        r.raise_for_status()
        batch = pd.DataFrame(r.json())

        if len(batch) == 0:
            break

        all_batches.append(batch)
        offset += batch_size

        if len(batch) < batch_size:
            break

    df_311 = pd.concat(all_batches, ignore_index=True)
    print(f'\nTotal complaints pulled: {len(df_311):,}')
    return df_311

df_311 = fetch_nyc_311_full(year=2025)

  Fetching records 0 – 50,000...
  Fetching records 50,000 – 100,000...
  Fetching records 100,000 – 150,000...
  Fetching records 150,000 – 200,000...

  Fetching records 250,000 – 300,000...
  Fetching records 300,000 – 350,000...

  Fetching records 400,000 – 450,000...
  Fetching records 450,000 – 500,000...

  Fetching records 550,000 – 600,000...
  Fetching records 600,000 – 650,000...
  Fetching records 650,000 – 700,000...
  Fetching records 700,000 – 750,000...
  Fetching records 750,000 – 800,000...
  Fetching records 800,000 – 850,000...
  Fetching records 850,000 – 900,000...
  Fetching records 900,000 – 950,000...
  Fetching records 950,000 – 1,000,000...
  Fetching records 1,000,000 – 1,050,000...
  Fetching records 1,050,000 – 1,100,000...

Total complaints pulled: 1,096,671
In [13]:
# Parse and clean the 311 data.
df_311['created_date'] = pd.to_datetime(df_311['created_date'], errors='coerce')
df_311 = df_311.dropna(subset=['created_date'])
for col in ['latitude','longitude']:
    df_311[col] = pd.to_numeric(df_311.get(col, np.nan), errors='coerce')
df_311['complaint_hour'] = df_311['created_date'].dt.floor('h')
for col in ['descriptor','complaint_type']:
    df_311[col] = df_311[col].fillna('').str.lower().str.strip()

print(f'Records with coordinates: {df_311["latitude"].notna().sum():,}')
df_311
