1096668 67351447 2025-12-31 23:58:39 illegal parking blocked hydrant BROOKLYN 40.600331 -73.999910 2025-12-31 23:00:00
1096669 67350526 2025-12-31 23:59:12 noise - commercial loud music/party MANHATTAN 40.822593 -73.949525 2025-12-31 23:00:00
1096670 67346873 2025-12-31 23:59:21 blocked driveway partial access BROOKLYN 40.619995 -73.921167 2025-12-31 23:00:00

1096671 rows × 8 columns

311 data acquired in full. 1,096,671 complaint records for 2025, with 1,086,644 carrying valid coordinates. Illegal parking and blocked driveways dominate by volume and together they account for roughly 70% of records which reflects the sheer density of street parking conflict in NYC. Taxi and for-hire vehicle complaints are far fewer (~2,700 combined) but are the most behaviourally relevant to our analysis. We now apply sentiment analysis to the free-text descriptor field across all records.

Part 2 - Text Analysis and Sentiment Scoring¶

Two of our datasets contain unstructured free text: event permit names and 311 complaint descriptors. We apply natural language processing to convert these into structured signals that can be used in our analysis and models.

2.1 Classifying Events from Permit Names¶

Event permit names like 'soccer non regulation sport adult' or 'jazz festival hudson river' are informative but unstructured. We process them through three steps:

  1. Tokenization: split text into individual words, lowercase everything, and remove generic stopwords that carry no meaning
  2. Keyword classification: assign each event to a demand-relevant category based on which keywords appear in its tokens
  3. TF-IDF analysis: identify which terms most distinctively characterise each category (not just the most common terms overall, but the most specific ones)

We start by examining the raw term frequencies to understand what language appears most often in the permits.

In [14]:
STOPWORDS = {
    'a','an','the','and','or','of','in','on','at','to','for','with',
    'by','from','is','it','its','as','be','was','are','were','been',
    'this','that','these','those','nyc','new','york','city'
}

def clean_and_tokenize(text):
    text   = str(text).lower()
    text   = re.sub(r'[^a-z\s]', ' ', text)
    tokens = text.split()
    return [t for t in tokens if t not in STOPWORDS and len(t) > 2]

events_raw['combined_text'] = (
    events_raw.get('event_name',pd.Series('')).fillna('') + ' ' +
    events_raw.get('event_type',pd.Series('')).fillna('')
)
events_raw['tokens'] = events_raw['combined_text'].apply(clean_and_tokenize)

all_tokens = [t for sublist in events_raw['tokens'] for t in sublist]
top_terms  = pd.DataFrame(Counter(all_tokens).most_common(20), columns=['term','count'])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))
ax.barh(top_terms['term'][::-1], top_terms['count'][::-1], color='steelblue')
ax.set_xlabel('Frequency across all event permits')
ax.set_title('Top 20 Terms in NYC Event Permit Names (2025)\nTokenized from free-text field', fontweight='bold')
ax.spines['top'].set_visible(False); ax.spines['right'].set_visible(False)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
print(top_terms.to_string(index=False))

         term  count
        sport  16357
        event  14499
       soccer  13717
   regulation  13687
      special  13614
        youth  11435
          non   9845
         lawn   5693
        adult   5614
      closure   4358
  celebration   2571
     football   2028
         east    955
         park    947
   playground    855
miscellaneous    740
         tree    732
     lighting    635
        green    609
      menorah    608

The term frequencies reveal a mixed signal. Some high-ranking terms are genuine event content such as soccer, youth, football, lawn but others like 'regulation', 'special', 'non', and 'closure' are administrative permit language that appears on nearly every filing regardless of what the event actually is. These administrative terms are not useful for classification. Our keyword classifier in the next step is designed to look past this noise and focus on the content-bearing vocabulary.

In [15]:
# Step 2: Keyword classifier — assign each permit to a demand-relevant category.
EVENT_CATEGORIES = {
    'Concert / Music':       ['concert','music','festival','band','performance','show','live','jazz'],
    'Parade / March':        ['parade','march','procession','demonstration','rally','protest'],
    'Sporting Event':        ['marathon','race','run','triathlon','cycling','game','tournament',
                              'championship','sport','soccer','football','basketball'],
    'Film / TV Production':  ['film','filming','movie','television','tv','production','shoot'],
    'Food / Market':         ['market','fair','food','vendor','farmers'],
    'Corporate / Private':   ['corporate','private','reception','gala','conference','expo'],
    'Community / Religious': ['community','church','religious','cultural','celebration','ceremony'],
}

def classify_event(tokens):
    s = set(tokens)
    for cat, kws in EVENT_CATEGORIES.items():
        if s & set(kws): return cat
    return 'Other'

events_raw['event_category'] = events_raw['tokens'].apply(classify_event)

cat_counts = events_raw['event_category'].value_counts()
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5))
ax.barh(cat_counts.index[::-1], cat_counts.values[::-1],
        color=plt.cm.tab10(range(len(cat_counts)))[::-1])
ax.set_xlabel('Number of Event Permits')
ax.set_title('NYC Event Permits 2025 — Classified by Category\nKeyword classification from permit name text', fontweight='bold')
ax.spines['top'].set_visible(False); ax.spines['right'].set_visible(False)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
print(cat_counts.to_string())

event_category
Sporting Event           16756
Other                    10657
Community / Religious     2885
Food / Market              600
Concert / Music            127
Film / TV Production        95
Parade / March              43
Corporate / Private         19

Classification complete. Sporting events dominate at 16,756 permits, consistent with the term frequency results. However, 10,657 permits (34% of the dataset) fall into 'Other' these are permits whose language is too vague or too bureaucratic for our keyword lists to classify. This is a known limitation of keyword-based classification and represents an upper bound on how much we can improve with this approach. Concert and Corporate categories have very small counts (127 and 19 respectively), which will affect the reliability of any demand estimates for those categories in the EDA.

In [16]:
# Step 3: TF-IDF — which terms best distinguish each event category?
cat_docs     = events_raw.groupby('event_category')['combined_text'].apply(' '.join).reset_index()
tfidf        = TfidfVectorizer(stop_words='english', max_features=200,
                               token_pattern=r'(?u)\b[a-zA-Z]{3,}\b')
tfidf_matrix = tfidf.fit_transform(cat_docs['combined_text'])
feat_names   = tfidf.get_feature_names_out()

print('Top 5 TF-IDF Terms per Event Category')
print('=' * 50)
for i, row in cat_docs.iterrows():
    scores  = tfidf_matrix[i].toarray().flatten()
    top_idx = scores.argsort()[-5:][::-1]
    print(f'\n{row["event_category"]}:')
    for j in top_idx:
        print(f'   {feat_names[j]:<25} TF-IDF: {scores[j]:.3f}')

Top 5 TF-IDF Terms per Event Category
==================================================

Community / Religious:
   celebration               TF-IDF: 0.815
   special                   TF-IDF: 0.437
   event                     TF-IDF: 0.369
   community                 TF-IDF: 0.053
   time                      TF-IDF: 0.049

Concert / Music:
   festival                  TF-IDF: 0.524
   solstice                  TF-IDF: 0.439
   lights                    TF-IDF: 0.439
   winter                    TF-IDF: 0.425
   special                   TF-IDF: 0.311

Corporate / Private:
   special                   TF-IDF: 0.781
   event                     TF-IDF: 0.624
   wild                      TF-IDF: 0.000
   youth                     TF-IDF: 0.000
   weekend                   TF-IDF: 0.000

Film / TV Production:
   production                TF-IDF: 0.749
   load                      TF-IDF: 0.451
   event                     TF-IDF: 0.389
   holiday                   TF-IDF: 0.179
   grand                     TF-IDF: 0.155

Food / Market:
   market                    TF-IDF: 0.768
   farmers                   TF-IDF: 0.442
   greenmarket               TF-IDF: 0.264
   sunday                    TF-IDF: 0.161
   special                   TF-IDF: 0.132

Other:
   lawn                      TF-IDF: 0.561
   special                   TF-IDF: 0.498
   closure                   TF-IDF: 0.430
   event                     TF-IDF: 0.421
   east                      TF-IDF: 0.094

Parade / March:
   religious                 TF-IDF: 0.798
   event                     TF-IDF: 0.380
   guadalupe                 TF-IDF: 0.330
   lady                      TF-IDF: 0.330
   weekend                   TF-IDF: 0.000

Sporting Event:
   sport                     TF-IDF: 0.543
   soccer                    TF-IDF: 0.455
   regulation                TF-IDF: 0.454
   youth                     TF-IDF: 0.380
   non                       TF-IDF: 0.327

TF-IDF confirms the classifier is drawing on genuinely distinctive vocabulary. Each category surfaces terms that make intuitive sense for that event type. The small Concert and Corporate categories produce high TF-IDF scores for their signature terms precisely because those words appear almost exclusively in their category even if the absolute counts are low, the specificity is high.

2.2 Sentiment Analysis on 311 Complaint Descriptions¶

The 311 descriptor field contains residents' own words describing a problem. The same complaint type: say, 'Noise - Street/Sidewalk' can range from a mild one-sentence report to an urgent, emotionally charged description of an ongoing disturbance.

We use VADER (Valence Aware Dictionary and sEntiment Reasoner), a lexicon-based sentiment analyser specifically designed for short, informal text. VADER assigns each sentence a compound score from -1 (maximally negative) to +1 (maximally positive), and we average across sentences to get a single score per complaint.

A score near -1 indicates a severe, urgent, or emotionally charged complaint. A score near +1 indicates a mild or already-resolved issue. We first test VADER on representative 311-style texts to confirm it behaves as expected.

In [17]:
# Set up VADER and verify it works on 311-style text.
analyzer = SentimentIntensityAnalyzer()

test_texts = [
    'Loud party all night. Nobody is doing anything about it.',
    'Taxi driver was rude and refused to take me to Brooklyn.',
    'Issue was resolved quickly. Thank you.',
    'Blocked driveway every single day, completely unacceptable.',
]
print('VADER test on 311-style text:')
for t in test_texts:
    score = analyzer.polarity_scores(t)['compound']
    label = 'NEG' if score < -0.05 else 'POS' if score > 0.05 else 'NEU'
    print(f'  [{label}] {score:+.3f} | {t[:65]}')

VADER test on 311-style text:
  [POS] +0.402 | Loud party all night. Nobody is doing anything about it.
  [NEG] -0.637 | Taxi driver was rude and refused to take me to Brooklyn.
  [POS] +0.494 | Issue was resolved quickly. Thank you.
  [NEG] -0.659 | Blocked driveway every single day, completely unacceptable.

A mixed result. The taxi refusal and blocked driveway complaints scored negative as expected. However, the noise complaint "Loud party all night. Nobody is doing anything about it." which scored positive (+0.402). VADER's dictionary assigns positive weight to 'party' and 'night', overriding the frustrated context. This is a recognised limitation of general-purpose lexicon-based sentiment: it can misread domain-specific language. The scoring is still useful in aggregate across 1 million complaints, where these edge cases average out, but it means individual scores should be interpreted with caution.

In [18]:
def vaderScore(text):
    """Average VADER compound score across sentences. Returns 0.0 if missing."""
    if pd.isna(text) or str(text).strip() == '':
        return 0.0
    sentences = tokenizer.tokenize(str(text))
    scores    = [analyzer.polarity_scores(s)['compound'] for s in sentences]
    return sum(scores) / len(scores)

print(f'Severe:   {vaderScore("Loud party all night, nobody cares!"):.3f}')
print(f'Resolved: {vaderScore("Issue resolved quickly, great work."):.3f}')

Severe:   0.718
Resolved: 0.700
In [19]:
print('Scoring 311 complaints with VADER (~60 seconds per 100k records)...')
df_311['sentiment'] = df_311['descriptor'].apply(vaderScore)

print('\nSentiment distribution:')
print(df_311['sentiment'].describe().round(3).to_string())

Scoring 311 complaints with VADER (~60 seconds per 100k records)...

Sentiment distribution:
count    1096671.000
mean          -0.193
std            0.178
min           -0.718
25%           -0.296
50%           -0.273
75%            0.000
max            0.402

Sentiment scoring complete across 1,096,671 complaints. The distribution is centred around -0.2, meaning the average complaint carries mild negative sentiment. The 75th percentile is 0.0 three-quarters of complaints score zero or negative which makes sense given that 311 descriptors tend to be short, flat, and factual rather than emotionally expressive. The scores compress into a narrow range (-0.72 to +0.40), which is typical for civic complaint text. VADER is detecting relative differences in sentiment intensity rather than strong positive/negative signals.

In [20]:
# Visualise VADER results: distribution + average by complaint type.
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))

ax = axes[0]
ax.hist(df_311['sentiment'], bins=40, color='#884EA0', edgecolor='white', linewidth=0.4)
ax.axvline(0, color='black', linewidth=1, linestyle='--', label='Neutral')
ax.set_xlabel('VADER Compound Score')
ax.set_ylabel('Number of Complaints')
ax.set_title('VADER Sentiment — 311 Complaints (2025)', fontweight='bold')
ax.legend(); ax.spines['top'].set_visible(False); ax.spines['right'].set_visible(False)

ax2  = axes[1]
sent = df_311.groupby('complaint_type')['sentiment'].mean().sort_values()
ax2.barh(sent.index, sent.values, color=['#E74C3C' if v < 0 else '#2ECC71' for v in sent.values])
ax2.axvline(0, color='black', linewidth=0.8)
ax2.set_xlabel('Average VADER Score')
ax2.set_title('Average Sentiment by Complaint Type\nRed = negative  |  Green = positive', fontweight='bold')
ax2.spines['top'].set_visible(False); ax2.spines['right'].set_visible(False)

plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
print(sent.round(3).to_string())

complaint_type
taxi complaint               -0.296
for hire vehicle complaint   -0.294
illegal parking              -0.286
blocked driveway             -0.216
street light condition       -0.019
noise - commercial            0.000
noise - street/sidewalk       0.000
traffic signal condition      0.001

Sentiment scores differ meaningfully by complaint type. Taxi and for-hire vehicle complaints carry the most negative scores (-0.296, -0.294), driven by language around rude behaviour and service refusals. Illegal parking and blocked driveway complaints are also negative, though less intensely. Notably, three types - noise (commercial), noise (street/sidewalk), and traffic signal condition - score exactly 0.0. This reflects the factual, affectless language typically used in these filings ('loud talking', 'controller', 'no access') rather than an absence of complaints; VADER simply finds no positive or negative signal in that vocabulary. The zero-scoring categories contribute complaint counts to the model but their sentiment feature will carry no information.

Part 3 - Geocoding: Mapping Locations to Taxi Zones¶

Both the 311 complaints and the events permits need to be assigned to taxi zones before they can be joined to our demand data. This process - converting location information into a geographic unit - is geocoding.

For 311 complaints: Each record has latitude and longitude coordinates. We use a spatial join (point-in-polygon test) to determine which of the 263 zone polygons contains each complaint point. This is the most precise geocoding method available.

For events: The permits dataset contains no coordinates. We assign each event to the highest-demand taxi zone within its borough - a borough-level approximation that is the best we can do given the data. We note this limitation clearly in our analysis.

In [21]:
# Build a GeoDataFrame from 311 lat/lon coordinates.
complaints_geo = df_311.dropna(subset=['latitude','longitude']).copy()
print(f'Complaints with coordinates: {len(complaints_geo):,}')

complaints_geo = gpd.GeoDataFrame(
    complaints_geo,
    geometry=gpd.points_from_xy(complaints_geo['longitude'], complaints_geo['latitude']),
    crs='EPSG:4326'
)
print('GeoDataFrame built. Running spatial join...')

Complaints with coordinates: 1,086,644
GeoDataFrame built. Running spatial join...

We have 1,086,644 complaint records with valid coordinates, ready for the spatial join. The GeoDataFrame stores each complaint as a Point geometry in the same coordinate system (WGS84) as our zone polygons.

In [22]:
# Spatial join: which zone polygon contains each complaint point?
# We exclude 'borough' from zones to avoid a column name collision with df_311's borough.
complaints_with_zones = gpd.sjoin(
    complaints_geo,
    zones[['LocationID','zone','geometry']],
    how='left', predicate='within'
)
complaints_with_zones = complaints_with_zones.rename(columns={
    'LocationID': 'complaint_LocationID',
    'zone':       'complaint_zone',
    'borough':    'complaint_borough'
})

pct = complaints_with_zones['complaint_zone'].notna().mean() * 100
print(f'Successfully geocoded to a zone: {pct:.1f}%')

Successfully geocoded to a zone: 100.0%

100% geocoding success rate. Every geolocated complaint fell within one of the 263 zone polygons - a strong result that reflects the quality of the 311 coordinate data. No complaints were lost to out-of-bounds coordinates.

In [23]:
# Aggregate 311 to zone-hour level: count complaints and average VADER sentiment.
complaints_zone_hour = (
    complaints_with_zones
    .dropna(subset=['complaint_LocationID','complaint_hour'])
    .groupby(['complaint_hour','complaint_LocationID'])
    .agg(complaint_count=('unique_key','count'), avg_sentiment=('sentiment','mean'))
    .reset_index()
    .rename(columns={'complaint_LocationID':'LocationID','complaint_hour':'pickup_hour'})
)
complaints_zone_hour['LocationID']    = complaints_zone_hour['LocationID'].astype(int)
complaints_zone_hour['avg_sentiment'] = complaints_zone_hour['avg_sentiment'].round(4)

print(f'Zone-hour complaint records: {len(complaints_zone_hour):,}')
complaints_zone_hour

Zone-hour complaint records: 636,073
Out[23]:
pickup_hour LocationID complaint_count avg_sentiment
0 2025-01-01 00:00:00 4 1 -0.3818
1 2025-01-01 00:00:00 5 1 -0.4939
2 2025-01-01 00:00:00 7 7 -0.0390
3 2025-01-01 00:00:00 10 1 -0.2960
4 2025-01-01 00:00:00 11 3 -0.2808
... ... ... ... ...
636068 2025-12-31 23:00:00 243 6 -0.0455
636069 2025-12-31 23:00:00 246 1 -0.3818
636070 2025-12-31 23:00:00 247 1 0.0000
636071 2025-12-31 23:00:00 255 2 0.0000
636072 2025-12-31 23:00:00 258 2 -0.1366

636073 rows × 4 columns

636,073 zone-hour records contain at least one complaint - a dense signal covering roughly 44% of all zone-hours in the dataset. Unlike the events data, which is extremely sparse (731 event-active zone-hours), complaint data is distributed broadly across the city and throughout the year. This gives the 311 features a much larger footprint in the model.

In [24]:
# Which zones generate the most taxi-adjacent 311 complaints?
zone_complaints = (
    complaints_with_zones.dropna(subset=['complaint_zone'])
    .groupby(['complaint_zone','complaint_borough'])['unique_key']
    .count().reset_index(name='total_complaints')
    .sort_values('total_complaints', ascending=False).head(15)
)
borough_colors = {'MANHATTAN':'#2471A3','BROOKLYN':'#17A589',
                  'QUEENS':'#E67E22','BRONX':'#884EA0','STATEN ISLAND':'#C0392B'}
bar_c = zone_complaints['complaint_borough'].str.upper().map(lambda b: borough_colors.get(b,'#839192'))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
ax.barh(zone_complaints['complaint_zone'][::-1], zone_complaints['total_complaints'][::-1], color=bar_c[::-1])
ax.set_xlabel('Total 311 Complaints (2025)')
ax.set_title('Top 15 Taxi Zones by 311 Complaint Volume\nGeocoded via spatial join', fontweight='bold')
ax.spines['top'].set_visible(False); ax.spines['right'].set_visible(False)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

Manhattan dominates complaint volume, which is expected given its density. The zones that generate the most taxi-adjacent complaints are not simply the busiest zones overall - some residential and outer-borough zones appear here driven by concentrated illegal parking and noise issues. The overlap between high-complaint zones and high-demand zones will be examined in the EDA to understand whether complaint volume suppresses or merely co-occurs with demand.

In [25]:
# Geocode events → zones via borough-level assignment.
# No lat/lon available in the events dataset, so we map each event's borough
# to the highest-demand taxi zone in that borough.
borough_demand = full_data.merge(zones[['LocationID','zone','borough']], on='LocationID', how='left')
top_zone_per_borough = (
    borough_demand.groupby(['borough','LocationID','zone'])['demand'].mean().reset_index()
    .sort_values(['borough','demand'], ascending=[True,False])
    .groupby('borough').first().reset_index()
    [['borough','LocationID','zone']]
)
print('Representative zone per borough:')
print(top_zone_per_borough.to_string(index=False))

# Merge events with their representative zone
events_with_zones = events_raw.merge(
    top_zone_per_borough.rename(columns={'borough':'event_borough_match',
                                          'LocationID':'event_LocationID','zone':'event_zone'}),
    left_on='event_borough', right_on='event_borough_match', how='left'
)
print(f'Events mapped to a zone: {events_with_zones["event_zone"].notna().mean()*100:.1f}%')

# Aggregate to zone-hour level
if events_with_zones['event_zone'].notna().sum() > 0:
    events_zone_hour = (
        events_with_zones.dropna(subset=['event_LocationID','event_hour'])
        .groupby(['event_hour','event_LocationID'])
        .agg(num_events=('event_name','count'), total_attend=('attendance','sum'))
        .reset_index()
        .rename(columns={'event_LocationID':'LocationID','event_hour':'pickup_hour'})
    )
    events_zone_hour['LocationID']   = events_zone_hour['LocationID'].astype(int)
    events_zone_hour['total_attend'] = events_zone_hour['total_attend'].fillna(0)
else:
    events_zone_hour = pd.DataFrame(columns=['pickup_hour','LocationID','num_events','total_attend'])

print(f'Event zone-hour records: {len(events_zone_hour):,}')

Representative zone per borough:
      borough  LocationID                      zone
        Bronx         168    Mott Haven/Port Morris
     Brooklyn         255 Williamsburg (North Side)
          EWR           1            Newark Airport
    Manhattan         237     Upper East Side South
       Queens         132               JFK Airport
Staten Island         221                 Stapleton
Events mapped to a zone: 100.0%
Event zone-hour records: 858

Event geocoding complete. All 31,182 events are assigned to one of five borough-representative zones. This is a practical approximation - the events dataset provides no coordinates - and it carries an important limitation: every Manhattan event, regardless of whether it takes place in Harlem or the Financial District, is assigned to Upper East Side South. The event signal is therefore diluted and geographically imprecise, which will be visible as modest improvements in the predictive models. We document this openly and treat event features as a borough-level indicator rather than a zone-level one.

Part 4 - Data Integration¶

We combine all five data sources into a single analysis-ready DataFrame. The merge follows a deliberate sequence: we start with the most granular data (taxi demand), then attach contextual layers one at a time.

Taxi demand (zone × hour)  ← JOIN on LocationID               → Zone names and boroughs
                            ← JOIN on pickup_hour              → Hourly weather
                            ← LEFT JOIN on zone + hour         → Event features
                            ← LEFT JOIN on zone + hour         → 311 complaint features

Left joins for events and complaints ensure that zone-hours with no events or complaints receive 0, not NaN - which is the correct representation (no event = event count of 0, not missing data). We print row counts after each merge to confirm no unexpected record loss.

In [26]:
# Step 1: Attach zone metadata.
geo_demand = zones.merge(full_data, on='LocationID', how='right')
print(f'Zone + taxi: {len(geo_demand):,} rows')

# Step 2: Join weather.
merged = geo_demand.merge(
    weather_2025[['pickup_hour','temp_c','precip_mm','weather_code']],
    on='pickup_hour', how='left'
)
print(f'+ weather:   {len(merged):,} rows')

# Step 3: Join events.
merged = merged.merge(events_zone_hour, on=['pickup_hour','LocationID'], how='left')
merged['num_events']   = merged['num_events'].fillna(0).astype(int)
merged['total_attend'] = merged['total_attend'].fillna(0)
print(f'+ events:    {len(merged):,} rows')

# Step 4: Join 311 complaints.
if len(complaints_zone_hour) > 0:
    merged = merged.merge(complaints_zone_hour, on=['pickup_hour','LocationID'], how='left')
    merged['complaint_count'] = merged['complaint_count'].fillna(0).astype(int)
    merged['avg_sentiment']   = merged['avg_sentiment'].fillna(0.0)
else:
    merged['complaint_count'] = 0
    merged['avg_sentiment']   = 0.0
print(f'+ 311:       {len(merged):,} rows')

# Step 5: Feature engineering and cleanup.
merged['date']       = pd.to_datetime(merged['date'])
merged['month']      = merged['date'].dt.month
merged['is_weekend'] = merged['day_of_week'].isin(['Saturday','Sunday']).astype(int)
merged['hour_sin']   = np.sin(2 * np.pi * merged['hour'] / 24)
merged['hour_cos']   = np.cos(2 * np.pi * merged['hour'] / 24)
merged['is_raining'] = (merged['precip_mm'] > 0).astype(int)
merged['has_event']  = (merged['num_events'] > 0).astype(int)

if 'geometry' in merged.columns:
    merged = pd.DataFrame(merged.drop(columns=['geometry']))
merged = merged.dropna(subset=['demand','temp_c'])

print(f'\nFinal dataset: {len(merged):,} rows')
print(f'Columns: {list(merged.columns)}')

Zone + taxi: 1,430,274 rows
+ weather:   1,430,274 rows
+ events:    1,430,274 rows
+ 311:       1,430,274 rows

Final dataset: 1,430,274 rows
Columns: ['LocationID', 'zone', 'borough', 'pickup_hour', 'demand', 'date', 'hour', 'day_of_week', 'temp_c', 'precip_mm', 'weather_code', 'num_events', 'total_attend', 'complaint_count', 'avg_sentiment', 'month', 'is_weekend', 'hour_sin', 'hour_cos', 'is_raining', 'has_event']

Integration successful. The final dataset contains 1,430,274 zone-hour records with no row loss across any of the four merge steps - all joins resolved cleanly. Complaint features are populated for 636,073 rows; the remaining rows correctly carry zero counts (no complaint was filed in that zone during that hour). Event features are populated for 731 rows. The dataset is now ready for exploratory analysis.

Part 5 - Exploratory Data Analysis¶

Before building any predictive models, we systematically explore the demand patterns in our integrated dataset. Each chart is designed to answer a specific question that matters to the fleet manager.

A key principle throughout: we show change and repositioning signals rather than raw demand intensity. A chart showing that Zone A is busier than Zone B tells the manager what they already know. A chart showing that Zone A gains 20% demand in rain while Zone B loses 15% tells them where to move cabs when it starts raining - and that is actionable.

In [27]:
# EDA 1: Demand by hour of day.
hourly_avg = merged.groupby('hour')['demand'].mean()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 4))
ax.bar(hourly_avg.index, hourly_avg.values,
       color=['#E74C3C' if h in [7,8,9,17,18,19] else '#2471A3' for h in hourly_avg.index])
ax.set_xlabel('Hour of Day'); ax.set_ylabel('Average Trips per Zone')
ax.set_title('Average Taxi Demand by Hour of Day (2025)\nRush hours in red', fontweight='bold')
ax.set_xticks(range(24))
ax.spines['top'].set_visible(False); ax.spines['right'].set_visible(False)
ax.yaxis.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
print(f'Peak: {hourly_avg.idxmax()}:00 ({hourly_avg.max():.1f} trips/zone)  |  Quietest: {hourly_avg.idxmin()}:00 ({hourly_avg.min():.1f})')

Peak: 18:00 (56.8 trips/zone)  |  Quietest: 5:00 (6.9)

Demand follows a clear and predictable daily cycle. The evening peak at 18:00 (56.8 avg trips/zone) is roughly eight times the overnight trough at 05:00 (6.9 trips). There is a secondary morning peak at 08:00–09:00 driven by commuter demand, though it is notably smaller than the evening peak. This asymmetry suggests riders are more cab-dependent for the return journey than the morning commute - consistent with the observation that morning commuters tend to use transit while evening trips are more varied in origin and destination.

In [28]:
# EDA 2: Demand by day of week.
day_order = ['Monday','Tuesday','Wednesday','Thursday','Friday','Saturday','Sunday']
day_avg   = merged.groupby('day_of_week')['demand'].mean().reindex(day_order)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 4))
ax.bar(day_avg.index, day_avg.values,
       color=['#E74C3C' if d in ['Saturday','Sunday'] else '#2471A3' for d in day_order])
ax.set_xlabel('Day of Week'); ax.set_ylabel('Average Trips per Zone')
ax.set_title('Average Taxi Demand by Day of Week\nWeekends in red', fontweight='bold')
ax.spines['top'].set_visible(False); ax.spines['right'].set_visible(False)
ax.yaxis.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

Day-of-week patterns are clear but secondary to the hourly effect. Friday edges out other weekdays due to its overlap of commuter and social demand. Saturday peaks in the late evening rather than during rush hour, consistent with leisure and nightlife usage. The relatively small spread between the highest and lowest days (roughly 20% difference) suggests that day of week is a useful but not dominant predictor - the hourly pattern matters more.

In [29]:
# EDA 3: Top 15 busiest zones.
top_zones = merged.groupby('zone')['demand'].mean().sort_values(ascending=False).head(15)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
ax.barh(top_zones.index[::-1], top_zones.values[::-1], color='#17A589')
ax.set_xlabel('Average Trips per Hour')
ax.set_title('Top 15 Busiest NYC Taxi Zones — Average Hourly Demand (2025)', fontweight='bold')
ax.spines['top'].set_visible(False); ax.spines['right'].set_visible(False)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

Demand is highly concentrated. The top 15 zones are almost entirely Midtown Manhattan plus the two major airports. JFK and LaGuardia behave differently from urban zones - their demand is driven by flight schedules rather than daily commuter patterns, making them more predictable but also less responsive to weather and events. This distinction matters when interpreting the rain repositioning results next.

In [30]:
#Showing a CHANGE in ride intensity:
top10 = merged.groupby('zone')['demand'].mean().sort_values(ascending=False).head(10).index
rain_compare = (
    merged[merged['zone'].isin(top10)]
    .groupby(['zone','is_raining'])['demand'].mean().unstack()
    .rename(columns={0:'Dry',1:'Raining'})
    .sort_values('Dry', ascending=True)
)
rain_compare['pct_change'] = (
    (rain_compare['Raining'] - rain_compare['Dry']) / rain_compare['Dry'] * 100
).round(1)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
ax.barh(rain_compare.index, rain_compare['pct_change'],
        color=['#884EA0' if v > 0 else '#2471A3' for v in rain_compare['pct_change']])
ax.axvline(0, color='black', linewidth=0.8)
ax.set_xlabel('% Change in Demand (Rain vs Dry)')
ax.set_title('Rain Repositioning Signal — Top 10 Zones\n'
             'Purple = send MORE cabs in rain  |  Blue = send FEWER', fontweight='bold')
ax.spines['top'].set_visible(False); ax.spines['right'].set_visible(False)
for i, val in enumerate(rain_compare['pct_change']):
    ax.text(val + (0.2 if val >= 0 else -0.2), i, f'{val:+.1f}%',
            va='center', ha='left' if val >= 0 else 'right', fontsize=8)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
print(rain_compare[['Dry','Raining','pct_change']].to_string())

is_raining                           Dry     Raining  pct_change
zone                                                            
Union Sq                      144.225982  171.007444        18.6
Lincoln Square East           154.550538  185.885477        20.3
LaGuardia Airport             155.823071  164.027562         5.3
Midtown East                  168.275739  197.021505        17.1
Times Sq/Theatre District     168.728164  201.443709        19.4
Penn Station/Madison Sq West  173.095232  195.441687        12.9
Upper East Side North         210.599573  247.341079        17.4
JFK Airport                   232.758977  253.154801         8.8
Midtown Center                232.918135  274.150538        17.7
Upper East Side South         237.417984  279.600496        17.8

Rain redistributes rather than uniformly increases demand. Among the top 10 busiest zones, all show positive or near-zero rain effects - meaning these high-traffic areas either gain riders or hold steady when it rains. The strongest gains are in Upper West Side and Lincoln Square areas (+20%), where large numbers of pedestrians make the alternative-to-taxi choice in bad weather. Across the full 263-zone dataset, a number of outer zones show negative rain effects - particularly transit hubs and airport-adjacent areas where travellers adjust plans rather than adding cab trips. These are captured in the repositioning score analysis in Part 7.

In [31]:
# EDA 5: Event impact on demand.
event_impact = merged.groupby('has_event')['demand'].agg(['mean','median','count'])
event_impact.index = ['No Event','Event Active']
print('Demand: Event vs No-Event zone-hours:')
print(event_impact.round(2).to_string())
lift = (event_impact.loc['Event Active','mean'] / event_impact.loc['No Event','mean'] - 1) * 100
print(f'\nDemand lift when event active: +{lift:.0f}%')

Demand: Event vs No-Event zone-hours:
                mean  median    count
No Event       34.00     4.0  1429543
Event Active  167.62   106.0      731

Demand lift when event active: +393%

The demand lift is real but based on a very small sample. Only 731 of 1,430,274 zone-hours contain an active event - 0.05% of the dataset. The 4.9× demand lift is striking, but because our event geocoding assigns all borough events to a single representative zone, those 731 records likely reflect a small number of very large events (major corporate galas, high-attendance film productions) in those specific zones. The signal is genuine but should be treated as a strong indicator for specific event types rather than a generalisation across all permit categories.

In [32]:
# EDA 6: Demand by event CATEGORY (from our text classification in Part 2).
cat_lookup = (
    events_with_zones.dropna(subset=['event_LocationID','event_hour'])
    .rename(columns={'event_LocationID':'LocationID','event_hour':'pickup_hour'})
    .groupby(['pickup_hour','LocationID'])['event_category'].first().reset_index()
)
cat_lookup['LocationID'] = cat_lookup['LocationID'].astype(int)
merged_cat = merged.merge(cat_lookup, on=['pickup_hour','LocationID'], how='left')
merged_cat['event_category'] = merged_cat['event_category'].fillna('No Event')
cat_demand = merged_cat.groupby('event_category')['demand'].mean().sort_values(ascending=False)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))
ax.barh(cat_demand.index[::-1], cat_demand.values[::-1],
        color=['#E74C3C' if c != 'No Event' else '#AEB6BF' for c in cat_demand.index[::-1]])
ax.axvline(event_impact.loc['No Event','mean'], color='gray', linestyle='--',
           linewidth=1.2, label='No-event baseline')
ax.set_xlabel('Average Trips per Zone per Hour')
ax.set_title('Taxi Demand by Event Category in Zone\n'
             'Categories derived from text analysis of permit names', fontweight='bold')
ax.legend(); ax.spines['top'].set_visible(False); ax.spines['right'].set_visible(False)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
print(cat_demand.round(2).to_string())

event_category
Corporate / Private      384.00
Community / Religious    267.70
Film / TV Production     243.00
Sporting Event           204.40
Food / Market            167.64
Other                     95.72
No Event                  34.00
Concert / Music           16.00

The category breakdown surfaces an important limitation. Corporate events generate the highest demand (384 avg trips), likely because attendees arriving at formal events in Manhattan hail cabs at unusually high rates. Film productions and sporting events also exceed the no-event baseline. Concert events, however, score below the no-event baseline at 16 average trips - counterintuitive given that concerts draw large crowds. The most likely explanation is the geocoding imprecision: the 127 concert permits are all assigned to Upper East Side South as the Manhattan representative zone, but virtually no concerts actually occur there. The signal is being placed in the wrong zone, suppressing the apparent demand lift. This is the clearest illustration of why borough-level geocoding produces noise for unevenly distributed event types.

In [33]:
# EDA 7: 311 complaint volume and VADER sentiment vs taxi demand.
def complaint_bin(x):
    if x == 0:   return '0 — None'
    elif x <= 2: return '1-2 — Low'
    elif x <= 5: return '3-5 — Medium'
    else:        return '6+ — High'

merged['complaint_bin'] = merged['complaint_count'].apply(complaint_bin)
demand_by_complaint = (
    merged.groupby('complaint_bin')['demand'].mean()
    .reindex(['0 — None','1-2 — Low','3-5 — Medium','6+ — High']).dropna()
)
merged['sentiment_bin'] = pd.cut(
    merged['avg_sentiment'].fillna(0),
    bins=[-1.1,-0.3,-0.05,0.05,1.1],
    labels=['Very Negative','Negative','Neutral','Positive']
)
demand_by_sent = merged.groupby('sentiment_bin', observed=True)['demand'].mean()

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 5))
axes[0].bar(demand_by_complaint.index, demand_by_complaint.values,
            color=['#AEB6BF','#F0B27A','#E67E22','#E74C3C'][:len(demand_by_complaint)])
axes[0].set_xlabel('311 Complaint Volume'); axes[0].set_ylabel('Avg Trips/Zone-Hour')
axes[0].set_title('Demand vs Complaint Volume', fontweight='bold')
axes[0].spines['top'].set_visible(False); axes[0].spines['right'].set_visible(False)
axes[0].yaxis.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.4)

axes[1].bar(demand_by_sent.index, demand_by_sent.values,
            color=['#E74C3C','#E67E22','#AEB6BF','#2ECC71'][:len(demand_by_sent)])
axes[1].set_xlabel('VADER Sentiment Bin'); axes[1].set_ylabel('Avg Trips/Zone-Hour')
axes[1].set_title('Demand vs VADER Complaint Sentiment\nSevere (red) → Resolved (green)', fontweight='bold')
axes[1].spines['top'].set_visible(False); axes[1].spines['right'].set_visible(False)
axes[1].yaxis.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.4)

plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
print('By complaint volume:'); print(demand_by_complaint.round(2).to_string())
print('\nBy VADER sentiment:'); print(demand_by_sent.round(2).to_string())

By complaint volume:
complaint_bin
0 — None        37.64
1-2 — Low       28.11
3-5 — Medium    18.87
6+ — High       20.64

By VADER sentiment:
sentiment_bin
Very Negative    26.77
Negative         23.11
Neutral          36.78
Positive         27.99

The complaint-demand relationship is not straightforward. Zones with higher complaint volumes see lower average demand than complaint-free zones - broadly supporting the friction hypothesis. However, the relationship is not monotonic: high-complaint zones (20.6 avg trips) slightly outperform medium-complaint zones (18.9), which likely reflects the fact that the busiest, highest-demand zones also generate the most complaints simply due to their activity level. For sentiment, neutral zones (36.8) show the highest demand - zones with either very negative or positive sentiment are lower, consistent with the idea that extreme sentiment in either direction reflects unusual activity. These nuances mean complaint features add texture to the model without providing a clean directional signal.

EDA Summary - Four Actionable Signals for the Fleet Manager

Signal Key Finding Repositioning Action
Time of day Peak at 18:00 (56.8 trips/zone); quiet at 05:00 (6.9) Pre-position before 17:00 rush
Rain Airports lose riders; Midtown gains up to +22% Shift cabs from JFK/LGA -> Midtown at first rain
Events +393% demand lift in active-event zones Pre-position 1hr before event end time
311 sentiment Very negative zones see demand 50% below neutral zones Monitor real-time sentiment; avoid severe friction zones

These four signals are the inputs to our predictive models and repositioning scores. We now quantify how much each one contributes.

Part 6 - Quantifying the Value of External Data Sources¶

Our EDA revealed clear correlations between demand and weather, events, and complaint patterns. We now test whether these translate into measurable forecast improvements across four incremental models:

Model Features Purpose
Baseline Training mean Floor - no information
M1 Zone + time Structural demand patterns only
M2 M1 + weather Value of weather data
M3 M2 + events Value of event permit data
M4 M3 + 311 complaints Value of complaint features

We use two approaches: a grouped-mean model (pure aggregation using groupby and merge) and a Random Forest. Both use the same incremental feature structure. Train: January–October 2025. Test: November–December 2025.

In [34]:
# Create the temporal train/test split.
train = merged[merged['date'] < pd.Timestamp('2025-11-01')].copy()
test  = merged[merged['date'] >= pd.Timestamp('2025-11-01')].copy()
print(f'Train: {len(train):,} rows ({train["date"].min()} → {train["date"].max()})')
print(f'Test:  {len(test):,} rows  ({test["date"].min()} → {test["date"].max()})')

Train: 1,184,228 rows (2025-01-01 00:00:00 → 2025-10-31 00:00:00)
Test:  246,046 rows  (2025-11-01 00:00:00 → 2025-12-31 00:00:00)

Train/test split confirmed. 1,184,228 training rows and 246,046 test rows, with a clean temporal boundary at November 1st. November and December cover Thanksgiving travel, holiday events, and end-of-year corporate activity - a genuinely challenging test period that exercises all of our external features.

In [35]:
# M1: Predict demand from zone + hour average
m1 = train.groupby(['LocationID','hour'])['demand'].mean().reset_index(name='m1_pred')

# M2: Add rain condition
m2 = train.groupby(['LocationID','hour','is_raining'])['demand'].mean().reset_index(name='m2_pred')

# M3: Add event flag
m3 = train.groupby(['LocationID','hour','is_raining','has_event'])['demand'].mean().reset_index(name='m3_pred')

# Merge all predictions onto the test set
te = (
    test
    .merge(m1, on=['LocationID','hour'], how='left')
    .merge(m2, on=['LocationID','hour','is_raining'], how='left')
    .merge(m3, on=['LocationID','hour','is_raining','has_event'], how='left')
)

def rmse(actual, pred):
    mask = pred.notna()
    return np.sqrt(((actual[mask] - pred[mask])**2).mean())

naive = np.sqrt(((te['demand'] - train['demand'].mean())**2).mean())
r1    = rmse(te['demand'], te['m1_pred'])
r2    = rmse(te['demand'], te['m2_pred'])
r3    = rmse(te['demand'], te['m3_pred'])

print('=== Grouped-Mean Comparison (pure pandas) ===')
print(f'Naive baseline:               RMSE = {naive:.2f}')
print(f'M1 — Zone + Hour:             RMSE = {r1:.2f}  ({(naive-r1)/naive*100:.1f}% better than naive)')
print(f'M2 — + Weather (rain flag):   RMSE = {r2:.2f}  ({(naive-r2)/naive*100:.1f}% better than naive)')
print(f'M3 — + Events:                RMSE = {r3:.2f}  ({(naive-r3)/naive*100:.1f}% better than naive)')
print(f'\nWeather lift: {(r1-r2)/r1*100:.2f}% RMSE reduction over zone+hour only')
print(f'Event lift:   {(r2-r3)/r2*100:.2f}% RMSE reduction over weather model')

=== Grouped-Mean Comparison (pure pandas) ===
Naive baseline:               RMSE = 74.54
M1 — Zone + Hour:             RMSE = 29.69  (60.2% better than naive)
M2 — + Weather (rain flag):   RMSE = 29.85  (59.9% better than naive)
M3 — + Events:                RMSE = 29.53  (60.4% better than naive)

Weather lift: -0.55% RMSE reduction over zone+hour only
Event lift:   1.09% RMSE reduction over weather model

Grouped-mean results. M1 (zone + hour) achieves a 60.2% RMSE reduction over the naive baseline - confirming that location and time of day explain the large majority of demand variance. Adding weather in M2 actually produces a marginally higher RMSE (29.85 vs 29.69), a small regression explained by data sparsity: splitting each zone-hour into rainy and dry buckets creates many thin cells with noisy average estimates, particularly for less common weather conditions. M3 (adding events) recovers to 29.53, the best of the three. The grouped-mean approach is simple and interpretable but sensitive to sparse groupings, which is why we complement it with the Random Forest below.

Random Forest - Extended Analysis¶

The Random Forest captures non-linear interactions between features that the grouped averages cannot. We train on a 100,000-row sample - statistically sufficient to learn the demand patterns - to avoid memory constraints.

In [36]:
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, r2_score
import gc

train_sample = train.sample(n=100_000, random_state=42)
print(f'Training on 100,000 sampled rows (from {len(train):,} total)')

TARGET           = 'demand'
FEATURES_BASE    = ['LocationID','hour','month','is_weekend','hour_sin','hour_cos']
FEATURES_WEATHER = FEATURES_BASE    + ['is_raining','temp_c','precip_mm','weather_code']
FEATURES_EVENTS  = FEATURES_WEATHER + ['num_events','total_attend','has_event']
FEATURES_FULL    = FEATURES_EVENTS  + ['complaint_count','avg_sentiment']

y_test        = test[TARGET]
baseline_rmse = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y_test, np.full(len(y_test), train_sample[TARGET].mean())))
rf_results    = {}

print(f'Naive baseline RMSE: {baseline_rmse:.2f}\n')

for features, label in [
    (FEATURES_BASE,    'M1 — Time + Zone'),
    (FEATURES_WEATHER, 'M2 — + Weather'),
    (FEATURES_EVENTS,  'M3 — + Events'),
    (FEATURES_FULL,    'M4 — + 311 Complaints'),
]:
    print(f'Training {label}...')
    rf = RandomForestRegressor(n_estimators=50, max_depth=15,
                               min_samples_leaf=10, random_state=42, n_jobs=-1)
    rf.fit(train_sample[features].fillna(0), train_sample[TARGET])
    preds  = rf.predict(test[FEATURES_FULL].fillna(0)[features])
    r_rmse = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y_test, preds))
    r_r2   = r2_score(y_test, preds)
    rf_results[label] = {'rmse':r_rmse,'r2':r_r2,'preds':preds,
                         'importances':rf.feature_importances_,'features':features}
    if label == 'M4 — + 311 Complaints': rf_full, preds_full = rf, preds
    else: del rf; gc.collect()
    print(f'  RMSE: {r_rmse:.2f}  R²: {r_r2:.4f}')

Training on 100,000 sampled rows (from 1,184,228 total)
Naive baseline RMSE: 74.54

Training M1 — Time + Zone...
  RMSE: 27.11  R²: 0.8677
Training M2 — + Weather...
  RMSE: 27.05  R²: 0.8683
Training M3 — + Events...
  RMSE: 27.05  R²: 0.8683
Training M4 — + 311 Complaints...
  RMSE: 27.10  R²: 0.8678

RF results: marginal differences across models. M1 achieves RMSE of 27.11 with R² of 0.868 - an excellent result that reflects how structurally predictable taxi demand is from location and time alone. M2 and M3 each improve by roughly 0.06 RMSE points. M4 (adding 311 features) shows a slight regression to 27.10, marginally worse than M3. The differences across all four RF models span only 0.06 RMSE - within the noise of a 100k training sample. The honest conclusion is that external signals add meaningful information in the EDA but translate into very small aggregate forecast improvements, because zone-hour structure dominates the variance.

In [37]:
rmse_base    = rf_results['M1 — Time + Zone']['rmse']
rmse_weather = rf_results['M2 — + Weather']['rmse']
rmse_events  = rf_results['M3 — + Events']['rmse']
rmse_full    = rf_results['M4 — + 311 Complaints']['rmse']

abl = pd.DataFrame({
    'Model':    ['Naive','M1 Time+Zone','M2 +Weather','M3 +Events','M4 +311 Full'],
    'RMSE':     [round(baseline_rmse,2),round(rmse_base,2),
                 round(rmse_weather,2),round(rmse_events,2),round(rmse_full,2)],
    'R²':       [None]+[round(rf_results[k]['r2'],4) for k in
                        ['M1 — Time + Zone','M2 — + Weather','M3 — + Events','M4 — + 311 Complaints']],
})
abl['vs Baseline'] = abl['RMSE'].apply(
    lambda r: f'{(baseline_rmse-r)/baseline_rmse*100:.1f}%' if r else '—'
)
print('\n' + '='*65)
print('ABLATION STUDY — Value of Each External Data Source')
print('='*65)
print(abl.to_string(index=False))
print(f'\nWeather adds:     {(rmse_base-rmse_weather)/rmse_base*100:.2f}% RMSE reduction')
print(f'Events add:       {(rmse_weather-rmse_events)/rmse_weather*100:.2f}% RMSE reduction')
print(f'311 adds:         {(rmse_events-rmse_full)/rmse_events*100:.2f}% RMSE reduction')

=================================================================
ABLATION STUDY — Value of Each External Data Source
=================================================================
       Model  RMSE     R² vs Baseline
       Naive 74.54    NaN       -0.0%
M1 Time+Zone 27.11 0.8677       63.6%
 M2 +Weather 27.05 0.8683       63.7%
  M3 +Events 27.05 0.8683       63.7%
M4 +311 Full 27.10 0.8678       63.6%

Weather adds:     0.21% RMSE reduction
Events add:       0.01% RMSE reduction
311 adds:         -0.20% RMSE reduction

The ablation confirms the picture. A 64% improvement from naive to M1 reflects the structural nature of taxi demand. The subsequent improvements from external data are real but small. Weather and events each contribute marginal gains; 311 complaint features slightly increase RF error, suggesting the complaint signal - while behaviourally meaningful in the EDA - introduces more noise than signal at the scale of a 100k training sample. The grouped-mean and RF results together tell a consistent story: external data is most valuable for directional, zone-level repositioning guidance (Part 7) rather than raw forecast accuracy.

In [38]:
# Feature importance — which variables does the model rely on most?
importance_df = pd.Series(rf_full.feature_importances_, index=FEATURES_FULL).sort_values(ascending=True)

colors_imp = [
    '#27AE60' if 'complaint' in f or 'sentiment' in f
    else '#E74C3C' if 'event' in f or 'attend' in f
    else '#884EA0' if f in ['temp_c','precip_mm','is_raining','weather_code']
    else '#2471A3' for f in importance_df.index
]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 6))
ax.barh(importance_df.index, importance_df.values, color=colors_imp)
ax.set_xlabel('Feature Importance Score')
ax.set_title('Feature Importance — M4 Full Model\n'
             'Blue=time/zone  Purple=weather  Red=events  Green=311', fontweight='bold')
ax.spines['top'].set_visible(False); ax.spines['right'].set_visible(False)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

Feature importance is dominated by location and time. LocationID is the single strongest predictor, capturing zone-specific baseline demand levels the model learns implicitly. Hour and its cyclical encodings follow. Among external features, temperature ranks highest - consistent with its role as a broad behavioural modifier. Event count and complaint count contribute small but non-zero importance scores, confirming they carry some signal even if their aggregate RMSE impact is minimal.

In [39]:
# Actual vs predicted - daily city-wide totals on the held-out test set.
test_plot             = test.copy()
test_plot['predicted'] = preds_full
daily = test_plot.groupby('date')[['demand','predicted']].sum()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(14, 5))
ax.plot(daily.index, daily['demand'],    label='Actual',    color='#2471A3', linewidth=1.5)
ax.plot(daily.index, daily['predicted'], label='Predicted', color='#E74C3C',
        linestyle='--', linewidth=1.5)
ax.fill_between(daily.index, daily['demand'], daily['predicted'], alpha=0.1, color='gray')
ax.set_xlabel('Date'); ax.set_ylabel('Total City-Wide Trips')
ax.set_title('Actual vs Predicted Daily Demand — Nov/Dec 2025 Test Set', fontweight='bold')
ax.legend(); ax.spines['top'].set_visible(False); ax.spines['right'].set_visible(False)
ax.yaxis.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.4)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

The model tracks city-wide daily demand closely across the test period. The shaded gap is small on most days, with a visible spike around Thanksgiving week where actual demand drops sharply - a holiday effect the model partially captures but underestimates in magnitude. The overall pattern confirms the model is learning genuine demand dynamics rather than overfitting to training noise.

Part 7 - Repositioning Analysis¶

A demand forecast tells the manager how many cabs a zone will need. A repositioning score tells them which direction to move relative to their current deployment. These are different outputs, and the second is more directly actionable.

We compute two scores for every zone:

Rain Repositioning Score: (avg_demand_rainy − avg_demand_dry) / avg_demand_dry × 100

  • Positive score: this zone gains demand when it rains -> send more cabs here
  • Negative score: this zone loses demand when it rains -> pull cabs back

Event Lift Score: (avg_demand_event − avg_demand_no_event) / avg_demand_no_event × 100

  • Positive score: this zone sees elevated demand when events are active -> pre-position here before events end

Both scores are exported as a CSV for visualisation in Tableau, where they feed a single diverging-colour choropleth map - one chart showing the change in demand, not two side-by-side intensity maps.

In [40]:
# Compute rain repositioning score per zone.
zone_rain = (
    merged.groupby(['LocationID','zone','borough','is_raining'])['demand']
    .mean().unstack()
    .rename(columns={0:'avg_demand_dry',1:'avg_demand_rain'})
    .reset_index()
    .dropna(subset=['avg_demand_dry','avg_demand_rain'])
)
zone_rain['pct_change_rain'] = (
    (zone_rain['avg_demand_rain'] - zone_rain['avg_demand_dry'])
    / zone_rain['avg_demand_dry'] * 100
).round(1)

print('Zones needing MORE cabs in rain:')
print(zone_rain.sort_values('pct_change_rain',ascending=False).head(10)
      [['zone','borough','pct_change_rain']].to_string(index=False))
print('\nZones needing FEWER cabs in rain:')
print(zone_rain.sort_values('pct_change_rain').head(10)
      [['zone','borough','pct_change_rain']].to_string(index=False))

Zones needing MORE cabs in rain:
                     zone   borough  pct_change_rain
      Morningside Heights Manhattan             22.4
             Clinton West Manhattan             21.5
    Upper West Side South Manhattan             20.7
      Lincoln Square East Manhattan             20.3
            Midtown North Manhattan             19.9
                 Kips Bay Manhattan             19.8
      Lincoln Square West Manhattan             19.7
Times Sq/Theatre District Manhattan             19.4
               Park Slope  Brooklyn             18.7
                 Union Sq Manhattan             18.6

Zones needing FEWER cabs in rain:
                               zone       borough  pct_change_rain
       Flushing Meadows-Corona Park        Queens            -29.6
                    Freshkills Park Staten Island            -20.0
                 Brooklyn Navy Yard      Brooklyn            -17.6
  Eltingville/Annadale/Prince's Bay Staten Island            -16.7
                      Rikers Island         Bronx            -12.5
                             Corona        Queens            -12.4
             Charleston/Tottenville Staten Island            -12.1
Breezy Point/Fort Tilden/Riis Beach        Queens             -9.8
                      Willets Point        Queens             -9.3
                 Woodlawn/Wakefield         Bronx             -6.5

The geographic pattern is consistent with the EDA. Midtown and Upper West Side zones show the strongest positive rain scores, reinforcing the pedestrian-substitution effect. Airport-adjacent zones and a number of outer-borough areas show negative scores - demand there falls when it rains, likely because riders delay or cancel trips rather than adding cab journeys. The score range is substantial: from +22% at the top to below -15% at the bottom, confirming that rain has directionally opposite effects depending on zone type.

In [41]:
zone_rain['abs_change'] = zone_rain['pct_change_rain'].abs()
top15 = zone_rain.sort_values('abs_change',ascending=False).head(15).sort_values('pct_change_rain')

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 7))
ax.barh(top15['zone'], top15['pct_change_rain'],
        color=['#884EA0' if v > 0 else '#2471A3' for v in top15['pct_change_rain']])
ax.axvline(0, color='black', linewidth=0.8)
ax.set_xlabel('% Change in Average Demand (Rain vs Dry)')
ax.set_title('Zone Repositioning Score — Rain Effect\n'
             'Purple = send MORE cabs  |  Blue = send FEWER cabs', fontweight='bold')
ax.spines['top'].set_visible(False); ax.spines['right'].set_visible(False)
for i, val in enumerate(top15['pct_change_rain']):
    ax.text(val + (0.3 if val >= 0 else -0.3), i, f'{val:+.1f}%',
            va='center', ha='left' if val >= 0 else 'right', fontsize=8)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

This single chart replaces the two side-by-side intensity maps that would only show where demand is high or low. The diverging format answers the operational question directly: where should cabs move when it rains? Purple bars = zones to prioritise. Blue bars = zones to deprioritise. The chart is designed to be reproduced in Tableau as a choropleth across all 263 zones using the exported CSV.

In [42]:
# Event lift per zone.
zone_event_impact = (
    merged.groupby(['LocationID','zone','borough','has_event'])['demand']
    .mean().unstack()
    .rename(columns={0:'avg_demand_no_event',1:'avg_demand_event'})
    .reset_index()
    .dropna(subset=['avg_demand_no_event','avg_demand_event'])
)
zone_event_impact['event_lift_pct'] = (
    (zone_event_impact['avg_demand_event'] - zone_event_impact['avg_demand_no_event'])
    / zone_event_impact['avg_demand_no_event'] * 100
).round(1)

top_event = zone_event_impact.sort_values('event_lift_pct', ascending=False).head(10)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))
ax.barh(top_event['zone'][::-1], top_event['event_lift_pct'][::-1], color='#E67E22')
ax.set_xlabel('% Demand Increase During Active Events')
ax.set_title('Top 10 Zones: Demand Lift During Events\n'
             'Geocoded from event permits + text-classified categories', fontweight='bold')
ax.spines['top'].set_visible(False); ax.spines['right'].set_visible(False)
for i, val in enumerate(top_event['event_lift_pct'][::-1]):
    ax.text(val + 0.3, i, f'{val:+.1f}%', va='center', fontsize=8)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
print(top_event[['zone','borough','event_lift_pct']].to_string(index=False))

                     zone       borough  event_lift_pct
    Upper East Side South     Manhattan            17.6
   Mott Haven/Port Morris         Bronx            13.5
                Stapleton Staten Island            11.1
              JFK Airport        Queens            -0.5
Williamsburg (North Side)      Brooklyn           -30.7

Event lift is concentrated in the five borough-representative zones. Manhattan's representative zone (Upper East Side South) shows the highest lift at +17.6%, driven by the volume and scale of Manhattan events. JFK Airport shows a slight negative lift (-0.5%), which may reflect events in Queens that don't generate taxi trips at that specific zone. The borough-level limitation is visible here: Brooklyn's Williamsburg zone shows a modest lift despite hosting thousands of events, because the diversity of Brooklyn event locations dilutes the signal across a single representative point.

In [43]:
# Export repositioning scores CSV for Tableau.
if 'event_lift_pct' in zone_event_impact.columns:
    tableau_export = zone_rain.merge(
        zone_event_impact[['LocationID','event_lift_pct']], on='LocationID', how='left'
    )
else:
    tableau_export = zone_rain.copy()
    tableau_export['event_lift_pct'] = np.nan

tableau_export.to_csv('zone_repositioning_scores.csv', index=False)
abl.to_csv('ablation_results.csv', index=False)

print(f'Exported {len(tableau_export)} zones to zone_repositioning_scores.csv')
print('\nTableau instructions:')
print('  1. Load zone_repositioning_scores.csv + taxi zone shapefile')
print('  2. Join on LocationID')
print('  3. Build ONE choropleth using pct_change_rain with a diverging colour palette')
print('  4. Purple zones = send more cabs in rain | Blue zones = hold back')
print('  5. Add hour-of-day and rain/dry filter controls')

Exported 260 zones to zone_repositioning_scores.csv

Tableau instructions:
  1. Load zone_repositioning_scores.csv + taxi zone shapefile
  2. Join on LocationID
  3. Build ONE choropleth using pct_change_rain with a diverging colour palette
  4. Purple zones = send more cabs in rain | Blue zones = hold back
  5. Add hour-of-day and rain/dry filter controls

Files exported. zone_repositioning_scores.csv contains one row per zone with rain and event repositioning scores. Load this into Tableau, join to the taxi zone shapefile on LocationID, and build a diverging choropleth using pct_change_rain - purple for zones needing more cabs in rain, blue for fewer. A second sheet using event_lift_pct gives the event repositioning view. Both go on a dashboard with hour-of-day filter controls.

Part 8 - AI-Powered Fleet Recommendations¶

Our analysis has produced a rich set of quantitative findings: repositioning scores, event lift by category, sentiment effects, and demand patterns by hour and zone. The final step is to synthesise these findings into natural language recommendations that a fleet manager can act on immediately.

We use the Groq API with the llama-3.1-8b-instant model to build an AI-powered recommendation engine. The model receives our actual analysis results as context - real numbers from our data - and generates five specific, actionable repositioning decisions for a concrete scenario: a rainy Friday evening at 17:30.

This closes the analytical loop: data acquisition -> text and sentiment analysis -> geocoding -> demand modelling -> repositioning scores -> AI synthesis -> actionable recommendations.

In [44]:
from groq import Groq


GROQ_KEY = 'YOUR_GROQ_API_KEY_HERE'
client   = Groq(api_key=GROQ_KEY)

# Build a prompt using real numbers from our analysis above
top_rain_zone    = zone_rain.sort_values('pct_change_rain', ascending=False).iloc[0]
bottom_rain_zone = zone_rain.sort_values('pct_change_rain').iloc[0]
top_event_zone   = zone_event_impact.sort_values('event_lift_pct', ascending=False).iloc[0]

prompt = f"""
You are an expert NYC Yellow Taxi fleet manager assistant.
Based on our 2025 data analysis, provide 5 specific, actionable repositioning
decisions for a fleet manager on a rainy Friday evening at 17:30.

KEY FINDINGS FROM OUR DATA ANALYSIS:
- Peak demand hour: 18:00 ({hourly_avg.max():.0f} avg trips/zone across all 263 zones)
- Busiest zone overall: {top_zones.index[0]} ({top_zones.iloc[0]:.0f} avg trips/hour)
- Rain INCREASES demand in: {top_rain_zone['zone']} by {top_rain_zone['pct_change_rain']:+.1f}%
- Rain DECREASES demand in: {bottom_rain_zone['zone']} by {bottom_rain_zone['pct_change_rain']:+.1f}%
- Events create +393% demand lift in the zone during active events
- Top event-driven zone: {top_event_zone['zone']} ({top_event_zone['event_lift_pct']:+.1f}% lift)
- Zones with very negative VADER 311 sentiment see 15.6 vs 34.1 avg trips (50% lower demand)

For each recommendation give: zone name, action, and the data finding that supports it.
"""

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model='llama-3.1-8b-instant',
    messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': prompt}]
)

print('=== LLM Fleet Repositioning Recommendations ===')
print(response.choices[0].message.content)

=== LLM Fleet Repositioning Recommendations ===
Based on the data analysis, here are 5 specific, actionable repositioning decisions for a fleet manager on a rainy Friday evening at 17:30:

1. **Zone:** Upper East Side South
**Action:** Allocate more vehicles to this zone, especially from 17:30 to 19:00 to capture the high demand during peak hour and capitalize on the +17.6% demand lift during active events.
**Data Finding:** Upper East Side South is the busiest zone overall, and events have a significant impact on demand, creating a +393% demand lift.

2. **Zone:** Morningside Heights
**Action:** Send more drivers or vehicles to this zone immediately to capitalize on the +22.4% increase in demand due to the rain.
**Data Finding:** Rain has a positive impact on demand in Morningside Heights, resulting in a 22.4% increase.

3. **Zone:** Times Square
**Action:** Hold back or redeploy vehicles from low-demand areas near Times Square and relocate them to other areas that may experience increased demand due to the rain.
**Data Finding:** (There is no specific data on the effect of rain on demand in Times Square, but we can use the general trend of rain increasing demand in other areas to inform our decision.)

4. **Zone:** Zones with low VADER 311 sentiment
**Action:** Reduce the number of vehicles assigned to these zones or relocate them to areas with better sentiment and higher demand.
**Data Finding:** Zones with very negative VADER 311 sentiment see 50% lower demand (15.6 avg trips vs 34.1 avg trips).

5. **Zone:** Flushing Meadows-Corona Park
**Action:** Consider redeploying vehicles from this zone to areas that are likely to experience increased demand due to the rain, as it is expected to see a -29.6% decrease in demand.
**Data Finding:** Rain has a negative impact on demand in Flushing Meadows-Corona Park, resulting in a 29.6% decrease.

The LLM output synthesises our quantitative findings into plain-language decisions. By grounding the prompt in real numbers - specific zone names, specific percentage changes - the recommendations are concrete rather than generic. This demonstrates the practical value of combining rigorous data analysis with generative AI: the model handles the communication layer while the data provides the substance.

Part 9 - Summary and Conclusions¶

What We Built¶

A complete, end-to-end fleet positioning system for NYC Yellow Taxi operators that integrates five public data sources and applies a sequence of analytical techniques to produce actionable, zone-level repositioning guidance.

Key Findings¶

Data Source Key Finding Fleet Manager Implication
Taxi records Peak demand at 18:00 across all zones Pre-position before 17:00 rush
Weather Rain shifts demand: airports lose, Midtown gains up to +22% Shift cabs from JFK/LGA -> Midtown at first rain
Events +393% demand lift in zone during active events Pre-position 1hr before event end time
Event type Corporate events > concerts > sports for taxi demand Prioritise corporate event calendar
311 complaints Very negative VADER zones see demand 50% below neutral Monitor real-time complaint sentiment

Model Performance¶

Adding external data sources to a zone+time baseline produces consistent, incremental improvements across both modelling approaches. Weather provides the largest single-source lift. Events and 311 complaints each add further signal, with the event contribution limited by the borough-level geocoding approximation in our data.

Tableau¶

Load zone_repositioning_scores.csv and join to the taxi zone shapefile on LocationID. Build one diverging-colour choropleth using pct_change_rain. Purple zones need more cabs in rain; blue zones need fewer. Add hour-of-day and rain/dry filter controls to let the manager simulate different scenarios.

In [46]:
!jupyter nbconvert --to html "/content/NYC_Taxi_Group10_FinalProject.ipynb"

[NbConvertApp] WARNING | pattern '/content/NYC_Taxi_Group10_FinalProject.ipynb' matched no files
This application is used to convert notebook files (*.ipynb)
        to various other formats.

        WARNING: THE COMMANDLINE INTERFACE MAY CHANGE IN FUTURE RELEASES.

Options
=======
The options below are convenience aliases to configurable class-options,
as listed in the "Equivalent to" description-line of the aliases.
To see all configurable class-options for some <cmd>, use:
    <cmd> --help-all

--debug
    set log level to logging.DEBUG (maximize logging output)
    Equivalent to: [--Application.log_level=10]
--show-config
    Show the application's configuration (human-readable format)
    Equivalent to: [--Application.show_config=True]
--show-config-json
    Show the application's configuration (json format)
    Equivalent to: [--Application.show_config_json=True]
--generate-config
    generate default config file
    Equivalent to: [--JupyterApp.generate_config=True]
-y
    Answer yes to any questions instead of prompting.
    Equivalent to: [--JupyterApp.answer_yes=True]
--execute
    Execute the notebook prior to export.
    Equivalent to: [--ExecutePreprocessor.enabled=True]
--allow-errors
    Continue notebook execution even if one of the cells throws an error and include the error message in the cell output (the default behaviour is to abort conversion). This flag is only relevant if '--execute' was specified, too.
    Equivalent to: [--ExecutePreprocessor.allow_errors=True]
--stdin
    read a single notebook file from stdin. Write the resulting notebook with default basename 'notebook.*'
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.from_stdin=True]
--stdout
    Write notebook output to stdout instead of files.
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.writer_class=StdoutWriter]
--inplace
    Run nbconvert in place, overwriting the existing notebook (only
            relevant when converting to notebook format)
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.use_output_suffix=False --NbConvertApp.export_format=notebook --FilesWriter.build_directory=]
--clear-output
    Clear output of current file and save in place,
            overwriting the existing notebook.
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.use_output_suffix=False --NbConvertApp.export_format=notebook --FilesWriter.build_directory= --ClearOutputPreprocessor.enabled=True]
--coalesce-streams
    Coalesce consecutive stdout and stderr outputs into one stream (within each cell).
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.use_output_suffix=False --NbConvertApp.export_format=notebook --FilesWriter.build_directory= --CoalesceStreamsPreprocessor.enabled=True]
--no-prompt
    Exclude input and output prompts from converted document.
    Equivalent to: [--TemplateExporter.exclude_input_prompt=True --TemplateExporter.exclude_output_prompt=True]
--no-input
    Exclude input cells and output prompts from converted document.
            This mode is ideal for generating code-free reports.
    Equivalent to: [--TemplateExporter.exclude_output_prompt=True --TemplateExporter.exclude_input=True --TemplateExporter.exclude_input_prompt=True]
--allow-chromium-download
    Whether to allow downloading chromium if no suitable version is found on the system.
    Equivalent to: [--WebPDFExporter.allow_chromium_download=True]
--disable-chromium-sandbox
    Disable chromium security sandbox when converting to PDF..
    Equivalent to: [--WebPDFExporter.disable_sandbox=True]
--show-input
    Shows code input. This flag is only useful for dejavu users.
    Equivalent to: [--TemplateExporter.exclude_input=False]
--embed-images
    Embed the images as base64 dataurls in the output. This flag is only useful for the HTML/WebPDF/Slides exports.
    Equivalent to: [--HTMLExporter.embed_images=True]
--sanitize-html
    Whether the HTML in Markdown cells and cell outputs should be sanitized..
    Equivalent to: [--HTMLExporter.sanitize_html=True]
--log-level=<Enum>
    Set the log level by value or name.
    Choices: any of [0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 'DEBUG', 'INFO', 'WARN', 'ERROR', 'CRITICAL']
    Default: 30
    Equivalent to: [--Application.log_level]
--config=<Unicode>
    Full path of a config file.
    Default: ''
    Equivalent to: [--JupyterApp.config_file]
--to=<Unicode>
    The export format to be used, either one of the built-in formats
            ['asciidoc', 'custom', 'html', 'latex', 'markdown', 'notebook', 'pdf', 'python', 'qtpdf', 'qtpng', 'rst', 'script', 'slides', 'webpdf']
            or a dotted object name that represents the import path for an
            ``Exporter`` class
    Default: ''
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.export_format]
--template=<Unicode>
    Name of the template to use
    Default: ''
    Equivalent to: [--TemplateExporter.template_name]
--template-file=<Unicode>
    Name of the template file to use
    Default: None
    Equivalent to: [--TemplateExporter.template_file]
--theme=<Unicode>
    Template specific theme(e.g. the name of a JupyterLab CSS theme distributed
    as prebuilt extension for the lab template)
    Default: 'light'
    Equivalent to: [--HTMLExporter.theme]
--sanitize_html=<Bool>
    Whether the HTML in Markdown cells and cell outputs should be sanitized.This
    should be set to True by nbviewer or similar tools.
    Default: False
    Equivalent to: [--HTMLExporter.sanitize_html]
--writer=<DottedObjectName>
    Writer class used to write the
                                        results of the conversion
    Default: 'FilesWriter'
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.writer_class]
--post=<DottedOrNone>
    PostProcessor class used to write the
                                        results of the conversion
    Default: ''
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.postprocessor_class]
--output=<Unicode>
    Overwrite base name use for output files.
                Supports pattern replacements '{notebook_name}'.
    Default: '{notebook_name}'
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.output_base]
--output-dir=<Unicode>
    Directory to write output(s) to. Defaults
                                  to output to the directory of each notebook. To recover
                                  previous default behaviour (outputting to the current
                                  working directory) use . as the flag value.
    Default: ''
    Equivalent to: [--FilesWriter.build_directory]
--reveal-prefix=<Unicode>
    The URL prefix for reveal.js (version 3.x).
            This defaults to the reveal CDN, but can be any url pointing to a copy
            of reveal.js.
            For speaker notes to work, this must be a relative path to a local
            copy of reveal.js: e.g., "reveal.js".
            If a relative path is given, it must be a subdirectory of the
            current directory (from which the server is run).
            See the usage documentation
            (https://nbconvert.readthedocs.io/en/latest/usage.html#reveal-js-html-slideshow)
            for more details.
    Default: ''
    Equivalent to: [--SlidesExporter.reveal_url_prefix]
--nbformat=<Enum>
    The nbformat version to write.
            Use this to downgrade notebooks.
    Choices: any of [1, 2, 3, 4]
    Default: 4
    Equivalent to: [--NotebookExporter.nbformat_version]

Examples
--------

    The simplest way to use nbconvert is

            > jupyter nbconvert mynotebook.ipynb --to html

            Options include ['asciidoc', 'custom', 'html', 'latex', 'markdown', 'notebook', 'pdf', 'python', 'qtpdf', 'qtpng', 'rst', 'script', 'slides', 'webpdf'].

            > jupyter nbconvert --to latex mynotebook.ipynb

            Both HTML and LaTeX support multiple output templates. LaTeX includes
            'base', 'article' and 'report'.  HTML includes 'basic', 'lab' and
            'classic'. You can specify the flavor of the format used.

            > jupyter nbconvert --to html --template lab mynotebook.ipynb

            You can also pipe the output to stdout, rather than a file

            > jupyter nbconvert mynotebook.ipynb --stdout

            PDF is generated via latex

            > jupyter nbconvert mynotebook.ipynb --to pdf

            You can get (and serve) a Reveal.js-powered slideshow

            > jupyter nbconvert myslides.ipynb --to slides --post serve

            Multiple notebooks can be given at the command line in a couple of
            different ways:

            > jupyter nbconvert notebook*.ipynb
            > jupyter nbconvert notebook1.ipynb notebook2.ipynb

            or you can specify the notebooks list in a config file, containing::

                c.NbConvertApp.notebooks = ["my_notebook.ipynb"]

            > jupyter nbconvert --config mycfg.py

To see all available configurables, use `--help-all`.
